w:r.w:t: This document has been reviewed and approved for development.

This System Requirements Specification (SRS) defines the functional, security, performance, and user-experience requirements for the Campus Security Alert System (CSAS). The system is a web-based application designed to deliver reliable, real-time security alerts and incident management capabilities to all campus stakeholders.
The Campus Security Alert System provides a centralized platform enabling:
University students, staff, and security personnel to receive instant, categorized alerts during campus emergencies.
Security administrators to broadcast incidents, manage user roles, and monitor security patterns via dashboards.
Secure, authenticated access using existing university credentials with role-based permissions.
Comprehensive logging of all incidents, alerts, and user-reported concerns for operational analysis.

The system will be delivered as a responsive web application accessible on desktop and mobile browsers. It will interface with university authentication services and provide browser-based push notifications. Core modules include: user authentication, alert broadcasting, incident management, role-based access control, and analytics dashboards.
The system must provide secure, credential-based access with granular role management.
Users must register and log in exclusively using valid university credentials (student/staff ID and institutional email).
The system must support Single Sign-On (SSO) integration with the university identity provider.
Role-based access control must distinguish between: Student, Staff, Security Officer, and Administrator roles.
Administrators must be able to assign, modify, and revoke user roles via a management interface.
Sessions must automatically expire after a configurable period of inactivity (default: 30 minutes).
The system must support optional Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) for security personnel and administrators.
All failed login attempts must be logged with timestamp, IP address, and account identifier.
Alerts must be delivered reliably, instantly, and with precise categorisation to prevent confusion during emergencies.
Authorised users (Security Officers, Admins) must be able to create and broadcast alerts to all or specific user groups.
Each alert must be categorised by: Severity (Critical / High / Medium / Low), Location (campus zone/building), and Incident Type (fire, medical, security threat, etc.).
Alert delivery must be confirmed within 5 seconds of broadcast to all connected users.
Browser push notifications must be delivered to all subscribed users, including those not actively using the app.
Users must be able to report incidents directly through the application; reports must notify security personnel immediately.
The system must support alert prioritization so that Critical alerts pre-empt lower-priority notifications.
Alerts must remain accessible in a historical feed for at least 90 days.
The system must implement industry-standard security controls to protect sensitive student, staff, and operational data.
All passwords must be hashed using bcrypt (minimum cost factor 12) or equivalent secure algorithm; plaintext passwords must never be stored.
All input fields must be validated and sanitised server-side to prevent SQL injection, XSS, and CSRF attacks.
All data in transit must be encrypted using TLS 1.2 or higher.
Sensitive data at rest (PII, credentials) must be encrypted using AES-256 or equivalent.
The application must implement OWASP Top 10 mitigations across all endpoints.
API endpoints must enforce authentication tokens (JWT or OAuth 2.0) for all protected resources.
Rate limiting must be applied to login and alert endpoints to prevent brute-force and DoS attacks.
All system activity must be logged systematically; dashboards must empower security personnel and administrators to track patterns and make informed decisions.
All incidents, alert broadcasts, and user-reported concerns must be logged with timestamp, actor, and action details.
An immutable audit log must record all administrative actions (role changes, deletions, system config changes).
The analytics dashboard must display real-time and historical metrics: alert frequency, high-risk zones, response times, and incident types.
Security personnel must be able to filter, search, and export log data by date range, severity, location, and type.
The system must generate automated reports (daily/weekly/monthly) summarising campus security activity for administrators.
Heat-map or geographic visualisation of high-risk areas must be available on the dashboard.
The application must be fully responsive and function correctly on desktop (1024px+), tablet, and mobile (320px+) screen sizes.
The interface must be intuitive, minimising the number of steps required to broadcast an alert or report an incident.
Critical alert notifications must be visually distinct (colour-coded, audible cue) from lower-priority messages.
All key actions must be reachable within 3 clicks/taps from the home screen.
The system must comply with WCAG 2.1 Level AA accessibility standards.
Alert broadcast latency must not exceed 5 seconds under normal load conditions.
The system must support a minimum of 5,000 concurrent users without performance degradation.
Page load time must not exceed 3 seconds on a standard campus network connection.
API response times must remain under 500ms for 95% of requests.
System uptime must meet a minimum SLA of 99.5% during term time.
Automatic failover mechanisms must be in place to maintain availability during partial infrastructure failures.
The notification delivery pipeline must include retry logic with exponential backoff.
The system architecture must support horizontal scaling to accommodate increased load during major campus events.
Database design must support efficient querying as log volume grows beyond 1 million records.
Penetration testing must be conducted before production deployment.
Security patches must be applied within 72 hours of critical vulnerability disclosure.
The system must comply with applicable data protection regulations (e.g., POPIA, GDPR).
The following test scenarios must be completed and signed off prior to production release:
Verify push notifications are received on Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge.
Test notification delivery when the app is running in the background or the browser tab is closed.
Confirm notifications arrive within the 5-second SLA under normal load.
Verify that Critical alerts visually and audibly override Medium/Low notifications.
Confirm alert queue ordering is preserved under simultaneous multi-alert conditions.
Test all core user journeys on iOS Safari, Android Chrome, and desktop browsers.
Validate layout integrity at 320px, 768px, and 1440px viewport widths.
Confirm real-time alert receipt on all tested device/browser combinations.
Conduct automated vulnerability scanning (OWASP ZAP or equivalent).
Test authentication bypass, session fixation, and privilege escalation scenarios.
Verify input validation against OWASP injection test cases.
The Campus Security Alert System follows a three-tier web architecture:
Responsive web UI, alert widgets, dashboards, notification banners
React / Vue.js, Tailwind CSS, Service Workers
Auth service, alert engine, RBAC middleware, logging service, notification dispatcher
User data, incident logs, audit trail, alert history
WebSockets or Server-Sent Events must be used for real-time alert delivery to connected clients.
A message queue (e.g., Redis Pub/Sub or RabbitMQ) must decouple alert broadcasting from the web server to ensure reliability under load.
All environment secrets (database credentials, API keys, JWT secrets) must be managed via environment variables or a secrets manager; they must never be hardcoded.
The proposal for the Campus Security Alert System has been reviewed and approved for development, subject to addressing all critical requirements defined in this document.
Web-based delivery; university credential integration required
Campus Security Alert System — System Requirements Specification v1.0 — Group 19 — March 2026
